instruction: What is the CNN-LSTM-SW model and what problem does it solve?
output: The CNN-LSTM-SW model is a hybrid deep learning architecture for railroad anomaly detection using Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) fiber-optic signals. It combines three components: (1) CNN layers for spatial feature extraction across signal channels, (2) LSTM layers for temporal pattern learning across time steps, and (3) a Sliding Window (SW) post-processing step that corrects point-to-point misclassifications at class boundaries. It solves the challenge of processing large, noisy DAS datasets (214,524+ samples) from a 4.16 km fiber-optic instrumented railroad track to detect four condition classes: Normal Condition (NC), Train Position (TP), Anomaly Class 1 (AC1), and Anomaly Class 2 (AC2).

instruction: How does the sliding window post-processing step work in CNN-LSTM-SW?
output: The sliding window (SW) post-processing corrects CNN-LSTM boundary misclassifications, particularly between TP and AC2 classes. For each prediction position i, a window of configurable size considers neighboring predictions on both sides. A majority vote is computed: if the most common class exceeds a threshold fraction of the window, the prediction at position i is corrected to that majority class. This dramatically reduces isolated misclassifications while preserving true anomaly boundaries along the track. The SW improved correct TP classification by identifying 87 additional correctly predicted data points that CNN-LSTM alone missed.

instruction: What are the four condition classes in the DAS railroad dataset?
output: The four condition classes in the HTL loop DAS dataset are: (1) NC (Normal Condition) — background rail and environmental noise, comprising approximately 93% of all samples; (2) TP (Train Position) — acoustic signal generated by a passing train; (3) AC1 (Anomaly Class 1) — light defect such as a wheel flat; (4) AC2 (Anomaly Class 2) — heavier anomaly such as a rail joint or structural defect. The severe class imbalance, with NC dominating at 93%, makes SMOTE oversampling essential for balanced model training.

instruction: Why is SMOTE used in the CNN-LSTM training pipeline?
output: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) is used because the DAS dataset is severely class-imbalanced: NC (Normal Condition) comprises approximately 93% of samples while AC1 and AC2 are rare anomaly classes. Without rebalancing, a model would achieve high accuracy by simply predicting NC for everything, but fail to detect the critical anomaly classes. SMOTE generates synthetic samples for minority classes (AC1, AC2, TP) by interpolating between existing samples in feature space, creating a more balanced training distribution without simply duplicating existing samples.

instruction: What feature domains are extracted from DAS signals for model training?
output: Three feature domains are extracted from DAS multi-channel signals: (1) Time domain (TD): mean, Pearson skewness (mean minus median divided by standard deviation), and kurtosis (4th standardized moment) per channel. (2) Frequency domain (FD): band energy ratio (low vs high frequency energy via FFT), spectral kurtosis, and spectral contrast. (3) Time-frequency domain: STFT-based features including spectral mean, standard deviation, maximum, and median. The combined T&FD feature set achieved the best performance at 97% train position detection rate, outperforming TD-only (89%) and FD-only (88%) configurations.

instruction: What were the ROC AUC scores for each condition class?
output: The ROC AUC scores for the CNN-LSTM-SW model on the HTL loop dataset were: AC1 (Anomaly Class 1) = 1.00 (perfect classification), NC (Normal Condition) = 0.99, TP (Train Position) = 0.97, and AC2 (Anomaly Class 2) = 0.95. These results demonstrate near-perfect discrimination ability across all condition classes, with AC2 being slightly harder to classify due to its boundary overlap with TP conditions.

instruction: How does distributed acoustic sensing work on railroad tracks?
output: Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) converts a standard optical fiber cable into an array of thousands of virtual microphones using Rayleigh backscattering. A laser pulse is sent down the fiber, and the backscattered light encodes acoustic vibrations at every point along the fiber's length. The iDAS system (Silixa) used in railroad monitoring has a gauge length of 10m, sensing range of 45km, and sampling frequency up to 100kHz. Key advantages include: distributed sensing over tens of kilometers from a single interrogator unit, no electrical power required along the sensing fiber, immunity to electromagnetic interference, and simultaneous monitoring of multiple train events.

instruction: Why did the GRU model outperform LSTM for DAS signal processing?
output: The GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) model achieved 94% train presence detection rate versus 93% for LSTM on DAS railroad data. GRU outperformed LSTM for this application due to three factors: (1) simpler architecture with fewer parameters (GRU has 2 gates vs LSTM's 3 gates), enabling faster convergence on the large DAS dataset; (2) reduced risk of overfitting on the relatively limited anomaly class samples; (3) comparable ability to capture long-range temporal dependencies in the acoustic signal while being computationally more efficient. For real-time railroad monitoring applications where inference speed matters, GRU's efficiency advantage is practically significant.

instruction: What is Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)?
output: Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) is a directed energy deposition process that builds metallic parts layer by layer using an electric arc as the heat source and a metal wire as the feedstock. Key process parameters include wire feed speed, travel speed, current intensity, and shielding gas flow rate. Material properties such as porosity, hardness, and tensile strength are highly sensitive to these parameters. WAAM is used for aerospace and defense applications where large metallic components need to be manufactured with precise material properties. The process generates large amounts of sensor data (temperature, current, deposition rate) that can be used for intelligent process control.

instruction: How does the HMM-RL pipeline optimize WAAM manufacturing?
output: The HMM-RL pipeline combines Hidden Markov Models and Reinforcement Learning for intelligent WAAM process control. The HMM component models latent manufacturing process states from sensor observations, identifying three key regimes: stable deposition, thermal buildup, and cooling phase. Transitions between states are learned from temperature and current sensor data. The RL component learns a control policy that maps process states to optimal parameter adjustments (wire feed speed, travel speed, current, gas flow) to maximize material quality while minimizing waste. On live WAAM datasets at Georgia Tech under the Georgia-AIM grant, this approach improved material utilization by 5% compared to baseline control strategies.

instruction: What is the HTL loop dataset used in the railroad research?
output: The HTL (High Tonnage Load) loop dataset is a live railroad sensor dataset collected at the AAR/TTCI (Association of American Railroads / Transportation Technology Center Inc.) test facility in Pueblo, Colorado. The setup involves a 4.16 km fiber-optic cable instrumented railroad track with a DAS (Distributed Acoustic Sensing) system. The dataset contains approximately 310,000 rows with 4,160 channels — one per meter of track — recorded at 400Hz to 100kHz sampling rates. The data contains four condition classes with severe imbalance: NC (93%), TP (few percent), AC1 and AC2 (rare). This is real industrial data collected under a research collaboration between Georgia Southern University and AAR/TTCI.

instruction: What is parameter-efficient fine-tuning and why is LoRA preferred?
output: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) adapts large pre-trained language models to new tasks by updating only a small fraction of parameters instead of the full model. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is the most widely used PEFT method: it injects trainable low-rank matrices into the attention layers while keeping the original model weights frozen. For a 7B parameter model, LoRA with rank r=16 trains only ~4.2 million parameters (0.06% of total), reducing GPU memory from ~56GB (full fine-tune) to ~12GB, and training time from 40+ hours to ~2 hours. QLoRA extends LoRA with 4-bit quantization, reducing memory further to ~6GB, enabling fine-tuning on a single consumer GPU. This makes large model adaptation accessible without expensive infrastructure.

instruction: What is the Georgia-AIM grant and what work is being done under it?
output: The Georgia-AIM (Georgia AI in Manufacturing) grant supports the Partnership for Innovation (PIN) program at Georgia Institute of Technology. Under this grant, Arifur is working as a PIN Fellow developing ML prototypes to advance manufacturing optimization. Specific work includes: (1) HMM-RL pipelines for WAAM (Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing) intelligent process control, achieving 5% material utilization improvement; (2) AI-guided optimization of polymer film synthesis using ML surrogates and multi-objective design; (3) deploying AI-driven material design prototypes to operational manufacturing environments; (4) establishing MLOps best practices for manufacturing AI teams. The goal is to integrate AI and machine learning into American manufacturing facilities for improved efficiency and quality.
